Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 79 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving RIGHT 3 times:
- Black object: cols 30-38 (was 21-29, moved 9 columns right)
- Charcoal object: cols 24-29 (was 36-44, moved 12 columns left!)
Wait, the charcoal object also moved! It was at cols 36-44, now it's at cols 24-29. That's a shift of -12 columns.
And the black object moved from cols 21-29 to cols 30-38, which is +9 columns.
So when I move RIGHT, both the black object and the charcoal object move. The black object moves right, and the charcoal object moves left. They're moving towards each other!
Looking at the ASCII:
Row 15: 'ccBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbb'
Row 16: 'ccBSBBWBBWBbbbbbbb'
The black object is now overlapping with the charcoal object! The 'cc' at the start of row 15-17 is the charcoal object, and the 'BBBBBBBBB' is the black object.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- The charcoal T-shape is at cols 24-29 (horizontal bar) and cols 24-26 (vertical bar)
- The black L-shape is at cols 30-38 (horizontal bar) and cols 36-38 (vertical bar)
So they're adjacent but not overlapping. The charcoal object is to the left of the black object.
But wait, the charcoal object moved from cols 36-44 to cols 24-29. That's a shift of -12 columns. And the black object moved from cols 21-29 to cols 30-38, which is +9 columns.
Hmm, this is strange. Let me recalculate:
- Initial black object: cols 18-26
- After 1 RIGHT: cols 21-29 (+3)
- After 3 more RIGHTs: cols 30-38 (+9 from previous, +12 from initial)
- Initial charcoal object: cols 36-44
- After 4 RIGHTs: cols 24-29 (-12 from initial)
So the black object moved +12 columns and the charcoal object moved -12 columns. They moved towards each other by 12 columns each.
This means when I press RIGHT, the black object moves right AND the charcoal object moves left. They're converging!
Now I see the pattern. The goal might be to move the black object so that it overlaps with the charcoal object, merging them into a single shape. Or maybe the goal is to position the black object so that it "completes" the charcoal T-shape.
Looking at the current layout:
- Charcoal T-shape: horizontal bar at cols 24-29, vertical bar at cols 24-26
- Black L-shape: horizontal bar at cols 30-38, vertical bar at cols 36-38
If I move RIGHT more, the black object will overlap with the charcoal object. Let me try moving RIGHT a few more times to see what happens when they overlap.
But wait, I also notice the yellow shape is still at rows 45-53, cols 51-59. Maybe the goal is to move the black object to overlap with the yellow shape, not the charcoal object.
Let me try moving DOWN to move the black object towards the yellow shape.
Actually, let me first check if moving RIGHT causes the black and charcoal objects to merge or interact in some way.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The black and charcoal objects are moving towards each other when I press RIGHT
# Let me try moving RIGHT more to see if they merge

# Current positions:
# Black: cols 30-38
# Charcoal: cols 24-29
# They're adjacent. Let me move RIGHT to overlap them.

for i in range(3):
    result = action(['RIGHT'])
    if result.get('level_completed') or result.get('done') or result.get('game_over'):
        print(f"Level completed/game over after {i+1} RIGHT moves!")
        break

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter more RIGHT moves:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40:
        print(f"  Black object: pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        print(f"  Charcoal object: pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"  Yellow shape: pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check ASCII
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Overlap area ===")
for r in range(14, 24):
    print(f"Row {r}: '{ascii_lines[r][20:46]}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
